description: The study of algebraic structures — groups, rings, fields, modules, and algebras — defined by abstract axioms. Unifies and generalizes arithmetic, geometry, and symmetry via a common algebraic framework.

concepts.name: Algebraic Structure
concepts.description: A set together with one or more binary operations satisfying axioms. The same set with different operations yields different structures. Abstract algebra studies these structures independently of the nature of elements.
references: Dummit & Foote, Abstract Algebra, Ch.1; Herstein, Topics in Algebra

concepts.name: Binary Operation
concepts.description: A function *: S×S→S assigning a unique element to each ordered pair. Closure: the result always stays in S. Associativity, commutativity, identity, and inverses are additional properties an operation may have.

concepts.name: Group
concepts.description: A set G with binary operation · satisfying: closure, associativity, identity element e (ge=eg=g), and inverses (∀g, ∃g⁻¹: gg⁻¹=e). Abelian (commutative) group: additionally gh=hg. Examples: (ℤ,+), (GL_n(ℝ),·).
references: Dummit & Foote, Abstract Algebra, Ch.1; Herstein, Topics in Algebra, Ch.2

concepts.name: Group Operation Examples
concepts.description: Concrete groups: (ℤₙ,+) cyclic; (ℤₙ*,·) units mod n; symmetric group Sₙ (permutations); dihedral group D_n (symmetries of n-gon); GL_n (invertible matrices). Cayley table fully describes a finite group.

concepts.name: Subgroup
concepts.description: H≤G iff H is non-empty, closed under the operation, and closed under inverses (subgroup criterion). Equivalently: ∀a,b∈H, ab⁻¹∈H. Trivial subgroups: {e} and G itself.

concepts.name: Cyclic Group
concepts.description: G = ⟨g⟩ = {gⁿ : n∈ℤ}; generated by a single element g. Finite cyclic of order n: ℤₙ≅⟨g⟩. Every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic. The cyclic groups are the simplest non-trivial groups.

concepts.name: Order of Group and Elements
concepts.description: |G| = number of elements in G. ord(g) = smallest positive n with gⁿ=e. In finite group: ord(g) divides |G| (Lagrange). Order 1 only for identity; cyclic subgroup ⟨g⟩ has order ord(g).

concepts.name: Coset
concepts.description: Left coset of H in G: aH = {ah : h∈H}. Cosets partition G; all cosets have the same size |H|. aH=bH iff a⁻¹b∈H. Index [G:H] = |G|/|H| = number of distinct cosets.

concepts.name: Lagrange's Theorem
concepts.description: If G is finite and H≤G, then |H| divides |G|. Equivalently, [G:H]·|H|=|G|. Corollary: ord(g) divides |G|; groups of prime order are cyclic.

concepts.name: Normal Subgroup
concepts.description: N◁G iff gNg⁻¹=N for all g∈G, equivalently gN=Ng (left and right cosets coincide). Kernels of homomorphisms are always normal. Normal subgroups are exactly those for which the quotient group is well-defined.

concepts.name: Quotient Group
concepts.description: G/N = {gN : g∈G} with operation (aN)(bN)=abN. Well-defined iff N◁G. |G/N|=[G:N]=|G|/|N| (finite case). The quotient 'collapses' N to the identity.

concepts.name: Group Homomorphism
concepts.description: A map φ:G→H with φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b). Preserves group structure. ker(φ) = {g∈G : φ(g)=eH} is a normal subgroup of G. im(φ) is a subgroup of H.

concepts.name: Group Isomorphism
concepts.description: A bijective homomorphism φ:G→H. Isomorphic groups are structurally identical. Automorphism: isomorphism G→G. Classify groups up to isomorphism: cyclic groups ℤₙ; abelian groups as products of cyclic groups.

concepts.name: First Isomorphism Theorem
concepts.description: If φ:G→H is a homomorphism, then G/ker(φ) ≅ im(φ). The quotient by the kernel is isomorphic to the image. Generalizes to rings and modules. Proved by showing the induced map Ḡ→im(φ) is an isomorphism.

concepts.name: Second and Third Isomorphism Theorems
concepts.description: Second (Diamond): If H≤G and N◁G, then HN/N ≅ H/(H∩N). Third: If N◁H◁G, then (G/N)/(H/N) ≅ G/H. Together with the first theorem, they describe the subgroup lattice structure under homomorphisms.

concepts.name: Group Action
concepts.description: A group action of G on set X: a map G×X→X satisfying identity and compatibility. Orbit of x: Gx={gx:g∈G}. Stabilizer of x: Gₓ={g:gx=x}. Orbit-Stabilizer theorem: |Gx|·|Gₓ|=|G|.

concepts.name: Burnside's Lemma
concepts.description: Number of distinct orbits = (1/|G|)∑_{g∈G}|X^g| where X^g is the set fixed by g. Counts distinct colorings/patterns under symmetry. Key tool in combinatorial enumeration (Polya counting).

concepts.name: Sylow Theorems
concepts.description: For |G|=pᵐ·k (p prime, p∤k): (1) Sylow p-subgroups of order pᵐ exist; (2) they are all conjugate; (3) their number nₚ satisfies nₚ|k and nₚ≡1(mod p). Used to classify groups of given order.

concepts.name: Symmetric Group
concepts.description: Sₙ = all bijections on {1,…,n}; |Sₙ|=n!. Every finite group embeds in Sₙ (Cayley's theorem). Cycle notation; cycle structure determines conjugacy class. Transpositions generate Sₙ.

concepts.name: Alternating Group
concepts.description: Aₙ = even permutations (product of even number of transpositions); |Aₙ|=n!/2. A normal subgroup of Sₙ. For n≥5, Aₙ is simple (no proper normal subgroups). Simplicity of A₅ implies quintic is not solvable by radicals.

concepts.name: Ring
concepts.description: A set R with two operations (+,·) where (R,+) is an abelian group, · is associative, and · distributes over +. Commutative ring: ab=ba. Examples: ℤ, ℤₙ, polynomial rings, matrices. Unity 1 if present makes a unital ring.

concepts.name: Ideal
concepts.description: A subset I⊆R such that (I,+)≤(R,+) and for all r∈R, i∈I: ri∈I and ir∈I. Quotient ring R/I is well-defined iff I is an ideal. Principal ideal ⟨a⟩={ra:r∈R} generated by one element.

concepts.name: Quotient Ring
concepts.description: R/I = {r+I : r∈R} with (r+I)+(s+I)=(r+s)+I and (r+I)(s+I)=rs+I. Analogue of quotient groups. First isomorphism theorem for rings: if φ:R→S is a ring homomorphism, R/ker(φ)≅im(φ).

concepts.name: Prime and Maximal Ideals
concepts.description: Prime ideal P: ab∈P ⟹ a∈P or b∈P. R/P is an integral domain. Maximal ideal M: no ideal strictly between M and R. R/M is a field. In commutative rings: every maximal ideal is prime.

concepts.name: Ring Homomorphism
concepts.description: A map φ:R→S with φ(a+b)=φ(a)+φ(b) and φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b). Preserves ring structure. ker(φ) is an ideal; im(φ) is a subring. Ring isomorphism: bijective ring homomorphism.

concepts.name: Polynomial Ring
concepts.description: R[x]: polynomials with coefficients in ring R under standard addition and multiplication. F[x] for field F is a Euclidean domain with polynomial division algorithm. Irreducible polynomials generate maximal ideals.

concepts.name: Euclidean Domain
concepts.description: An integral domain R with a Euclidean norm N:R\{0}→ℕ₀ allowing division with remainder. Examples: ℤ (norm = absolute value), F[x] (norm = degree). In EDs, the Euclidean algorithm computes GCDs.

concepts.name: Principal Ideal Domain
concepts.description: An integral domain where every ideal is principal (generated by one element). Every ED is a PID but not conversely. In a PID, every prime ideal is maximal and irreducibles equal primes. Structure theorem for modules over PIDs generalizes Jordan form.

concepts.name: Unique Factorization Domain
concepts.description: An integral domain where every non-zero, non-unit element factors uniquely (up to order and units) into irreducibles. Every PID is a UFD. ℤ[x] is a UFD (Gauss's lemma) but not a PID.

concepts.name: Field
concepts.description: A commutative ring where every non-zero element has a multiplicative inverse. Examples: ℚ, ℝ, ℂ, ℤₚ (p prime). The characteristic of a field is 0 or a prime. Fields are the coefficient domains of vector spaces.

concepts.name: Field Extension
concepts.description: K is a field extension of F (K/F) if K is a field containing F as subfield. Degree [K:F] = dim_F(K). Tower law: [K:F]=[K:E][E:F]. Extensions are simple (K=F(α)), algebraic, or transcendental.

concepts.name: Algebraic Extension
concepts.description: α is algebraic over F if it satisfies some polynomial with F-coefficients. Minimal polynomial mα(x): the monic irreducible polynomial of least degree satisfied by α. F(α)≅F[x]/(mα(x)); [F(α):F]=deg(mα).

concepts.name: Finite Field
concepts.description: Finite fields exist iff |F|=pⁿ (p prime, n≥1), denoted 𝔽_{pⁿ} or GF(pⁿ). Unique up to isomorphism. Multiplicative group is cyclic. Used in coding theory, cryptography, and combinatorics.

concepts.name: Galois Theory
concepts.description: Studies field extensions via their automorphism groups. Fundamental theorem: a bijective correspondence between subfields of a Galois extension K/F and subgroups of Gal(K/F). Resolves classical impossibility problems.
references: Dummit & Foote, Abstract Algebra, Ch.14; Stewart, Galois Theory

concepts.name: Galois Group
concepts.description: Gal(K/F) = {σ:K→K automorphism fixing F pointwise}. Group under composition. |Gal(K/F)| = [K:F] for Galois extensions. For 𝔽_{pⁿ}/𝔽_p: Gal ≅ ℤₙ (Frobenius generates it).

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory
concepts.description: For a Galois extension K/F: there is a bijection between intermediate fields E (F⊆E⊆K) and subgroups H of Gal(K/F). Correspondence: E↔Gal(K/E); normal subgroups correspond to Galois subextensions.

concepts.name: Group Inverse
concepts.description: For every element g in a group G, there exists a unique g⁻¹ such that gg⁻¹=g⁻¹g=e. Uniqueness proved from the axioms: if ga=e and gb=e then a=b.
